entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
Read about Canadian history from 1800-1900 through a variety of texts: engaging narratives, history spines, primary source documents, and historical fiction. These books, which offer a range of perspectives, will immerse students in the time period and allow them to see the world through the eyes of those who lived it. Students will view images and reference atlases and maps to set the people and events in their time and place. Update History Charts and Book of Centuries as appropriate for student level.

This topic is included in the following course(s): History: Grades 5-6 (Canada)

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content: This course is the same for students in grades 5 and 6. In grade 5, students may begin their Book of Centuries. It could be easily modified for students in grade 4, if combining.

rows.label: Canadian History: Grades 5-6

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

History: Grades 5-6 (Canada)
☐ Read the document in the Quick Links about History Timelines and Charts.

☐ We encourage you to pick a biography for each term to read in the afternoons. The Bookshelf has many recommendations.

entries.title: Term Prep & Teacher Tips
entries.content:
Canadian History: Grades 5-6
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune is a historical novel that recounts the brave rescue of sailors from the HMS Tribune by a thirteen-year-old boy known only as "Joe Cracker" to history. The author Jacqueline Halsey imagines a backstory for him, and parents may wish to pre-read the reveal of Joe's parentage on pages 145-146 in advance to decide what is best for their particular student(s), especially those on the younger end of the age range. While the circumstances of Joe's birth and the small boat he later uses in his rescue both caused the fracturing of a family, Joe himself and the boat are what ultimately mend and heal the remaining family members. Some may choose to read the story aloud to edit on the fly if needed and to encourage discussion. Others will be fine with the content as is. We encourage all to provide an inviting space for students to ask questions and share thoughts as they process what they are reading in their books. There is always the option of substituting for Belle of Batoche from Grades 2-4 or Jeremy's War from Grades 7-8.

books.title: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

blocks.text:
• Read through the lesson and gather any materials or find any necessary links. 

• If you have not already read the book, you may want to read the selected pages in advance.

• Connect to previous lessons by asking students to recall where they left off. 

• If students have difficulty recalling, teachers might try showing them a picture from the reading, sharing something the teacher remembers, or prompting them with the first sentence of the last reading. This will often refresh their memory enough to allow them to recall.

• If students still cannot recall anything from the last reading, teachers might consider continuing anyway (with a shorter reading this time) or repeating the previous lesson, depending on the reading and the nature of the book.

• Use a picture, map, or engaging question to pique the student's interest in the day's lesson. 

• If necessary, you may talk about one or two unfamiliar words that will be crucial to understanding.

• Read the day's passage out loud. When reading to pre-readers, point to the words as you read, even if it seems like they aren't paying attention.

• Move at the student’s pace for the lesson time rather than a particular number of pages. It is vitally important that teachers not try to ‘force feed’ a student more than they can consume in one sitting.

• Students who have a difficult time sitting still or listening may benefit from a fidget of their choice, such as a piece of putty or clay or an afghan; flexible positioning, such as a wobble seat or inverted sitting; or sensory modulation, such as a weighted vest or blanket or adjustment of light, temperature, or sound.

• Have students retell the passage orally.

• Alternatively, learners may use words, pictures, Legos, dolls, etc., to process and convey ideas in the manner most natural to them.  

• Teachers may take turns to model.

• Narration is a complex skill requiring the coordination of several parts of the brain. If a student struggles to narrate in general, with a specific subject or book, or on one day but not others, then this is an opportunity to learn. Observe when they struggle with this or any other activity today. Notice facial expressions and body language; listen to what they say about why it is hard or how they feel about it. Take note of these observations for later, tell them that you see how hard they are working, and then offer whatever support they need to end on a positive note. If necessary, take a break for the day.

• Give the student an opportunity to share thoughts on the lesson and any connections made.  

• To stimulate discussion, you could ask them what they thought about the passage or ask an open-ended question.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune; Canadian Student Atlas or map of Nova Scotia

➜ INTRO
The years 1800-1900 were a momentous century for the history of Canada. It saw wars, rebellions, large waves of immigration, new and expanded settlements, exploration to the west and north, and Canada's Confederation of 1867, with more provinces and territories joining in the decades that followed. This course uses the narrative history The Story of Canada to provide an overview of the major events of the years 1800-1900, and a historical novel, a picture book, and a graphic novel to immerse students in three distinct moments in time: life in a fishing village on Nova Scotia's Chebucto Peninsula at the turn of the century, the life of Tecumseh, a Shawnee chief and British army ally in the War of 1812, and the Confederation Conferences of 1864, where the wheels of Canada's Confederacy were set in motion.
The first book, Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune, tells the story of a thirteen-year-old boy who risked his life to save drowning sailors from the wreck of the H.M.S. Tribune, off the coast of Nova Scotia. This historical fiction novel imagines a backstory for the boy only known to history as "Joe Cracker," and it paints a detailed picture of what life was like in a small fishing village around 1800.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.1-13 "Wisps of fog" - "all about the ocean."

• Who are the characters you meet as the novel opens? How are they related to each other? What are they like? You may choose to keep a character list with some notes.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resources: Canadian Student Atlas p.41 or any other map (including online) showing Nova Scotia
Locate Halifax and the Chebucto Peninsula. Does your map show Herring Cove?

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: Explore Herring Cove
Watch a video tour of Herring Cove with Senator Stan Kutcher. If you're short on time, feel free to skip 1:58-2:54. This whole video could be saved for another point in the course, but it touches on many themes of the novel, so it's definitely not a video to miss!
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Please read the header pages for this course that list the page numbers for the sensitive content you may wish to preview.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from the opening chapters of the novel?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.13-23 "I broke my arm" - "Next job, Eli's shed."

• As you read, compare Joe's daily life to yours. For example, have you or anyone in your family ever broken a bone? How was it treated? How was Joe's treatment different? Or, what are your chores in the morning? Are they similar to or different from Joe's?

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.24-35 "Eli's derelict old shed" - "Nothing would burn today."
As you learn more about the characters, add to your list or chart.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What did you learn from last week's reading about Gram?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.36-47 "Buttoned into his oilskins" - "mend a holey rowboat."
Continue adding to your list of characters.

➜ VIEW
Learn more about what killicks are and how they were made.
∞ Video Link: Making a Killick Anchor
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING
Try making your own chicken and potato pasties, like Joe had in his lunch.
∞ Recipe Link: Chicken and Vegetable Pasties

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from last week's reading about the types of work Joe has to do?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.48-60 "Ten long days of tides" - "to the old shed."

• Earlier in the novel, we learned that Joe has never been to school. This was not unusual two hundred years ago, when children were often needed to work inside or outside the home to help support their families. Why do you think Joe has never been to school? How does he learn what he needs to know?

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from last week's reading about the mystery of the skiff?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.60-72 "The shed smelled" - "when he saw the skiff,' said Joe."

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.73-84 "The next evening" - "Joe really, really hoped so."

• How has medicine and medical care changed from Joe's time to ours? What are some differences? Are there any similarities?

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 8
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What details or events stood out to you from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.85-97 "Gram was better" - "It should all work out."
Remember to keep adding information to your character chart.

• Do you have any predictions for what will happen on Race Day?

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 9
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.98-110 "Joe ate his porridge" - "weren't meant to be."
Keep adding to your character list as you learn more about the character in the story.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 10
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you remember about Race Day from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.111-124 "Winter arrived that night." - "Frederick lagged behind."

• What has happened to the Tribune? Why won't the captain receive assistance from either other Navy ships or the local fishermen?

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Capture of La Tribune June 8th 1796, Thomas Whitcombe
Look at this painting by maritime painter Thomas Whitcombe (1763-1824) of the Capture of the Tribune in 1796 by the British Navy. The Tribune was originally part of the French navy before its capture. What do you notice in the painting about these types of ships called frigates?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Today's reading is a bit longer than the others, so you may want to leave extra time or finish the reading in the afternoon.

links.text: Capture of La Tribune June 8th 1796, Thomas Whitcombe

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 11
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from the reading last week about the Tribune's predicament?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.125-136 "It was toasty warm" - "He'd never know."
Remember to continue adding to your character list as you learn more about the characters.

• What do you predict is going to happen? Why do you think Gram told Joe his father's name at this moment?

➜ READ & DISCUSS
1\. Read an account of the wreck of the Tribune from Nova Scotia's Maritime Museum of the Atlantic. 
∞ Article Link: HMS Tribune - 1797
2\. Read a second account of the wreck, taken from contemporary sources. Numerous details from the novel appear in this account—can you identify some of them?
∞ Article Link: Tribune

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 12
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.

Questions will come from:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 13
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from the reading last time about the Tribune's predicament and Joe's plan?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.137-150 "It took three goes" - "'Knowing always changes things,' said Nettie."

• What was the result of Joe's rescue? Why does the town think of him as a hero? 
• How did the author use the few details that can be found in the historical archive about the wreck of the Tribune to expand and enrich the story?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Note the sensitive content on p.145-146.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 14
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What details were most vivid from the reading last week?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.151-162 "The next weeks passed" - "come from my imagination."

• What are Joe's impressions of Halifax? While Halifax is only fifteen kilometres from Herring Cove (and about a 20-minute car trip), this would be a great distance to someone like Joe in 1797.
• Why did Joe decide to stay in Herring Cove, and why do you think he chose a pair of corduroy pants as a reward? Why do you think that detail stuck out so vividly to the author?
• What did you think of the novel?

➜ VIEW
Look at images of the monument to Joe Cracker.
∞ Link: Joe Cracker Joseph Shortt HMS Tribune

links.text: Joe Cracker Joseph Shortt HMS Tribune

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 15
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you remember about what happened after Joe's rescue from the reading last week?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.162-169 "A woman bustled up" - "come from my imagination."

• Why did Joe decide to stay in Herring Cove, and why do you think he chose a pair of corduroy pants as a reward? Why do you think that detail stuck out so vividly to the author?
• What did you think of the novel?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP 
In order to finish the novel, today's reading is longer than the others, so you may want to leave extra time, finish the reading in the afternoon, or find another time to read the Author's Note and view the monument.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 16
lessons.body:
The War of 1812
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada; atlas or map of Canada

➜ INTRO
In Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune, when Joe gets a tour of a Navy ship, the sailor Jacko tells him, "We's at war, you know." The British Navy had been engaged in naval conflicts for much of the eighteenth century and continued until peace was declared in 1815 after the Battle of Waterloo. One of Britain's last major battles before 1815 was the War of 1812, which involved Canada. Over the next few weeks, we will learn more about this war and why it was significant to Canada's history.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.78-81 "Britain and the United States" - "British North America would stay British."
p.92 and 94 (skip page 93) "A few years later" - "into the North."

➜ MAP WORK
Using a map of Ontario or Canada, locate Queenston Heights (near Niagara Falls), Detroit, Toronto (called York in 1812), Montreal, the St. Lawrence River, and the Great Lakes (Lake Ontario, Lake Erie, Lake Huron, Lake Michigan, and Lake Superior).

➜ VIEW
1\. Spend some time looking at this engraving of The Battle of Queenston Heights. The original engraving this one is based on is attributed to Captain James Dennis, who fought in the battle. The Americans (in blue) are crossing the Niagara River to Queenston (the right side) from Lewiston (the left side).
∞ Image Link: The Battle of Queenston Heights

2\. Visit Brock's Monument at Queenston Heights. You don't need to watch the whole video, but the drone footage provides an amazing view!
∞ Video Link: Canada's Most Imposing War Memorial
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
Tecumseh (1768-1813)
Sir Isaac Brock (1769–1812)
War of 1812 (1812)

• EXTRA HELPING 
Watch a Heritage Minute about how the Grand River warriors helped to defeat the Americans at Queenston Heights after the death of General Brock. 
∞ Video Link: Heritage Minutes: Queenston Heights

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 17
lessons.body:
Tecumseh
⍞ Materials: Tecumseh

➜ INTRO
For the next three weeks, you will learn more about the life of Tecumseh, the Shawnee chief and warrior who allied with the British against the threat of invasion from the United States during the War of 1812. Tecumseh rallied other Indigenous peoples to side with him and had a vision of the ancestral lands being restored to First Nations tribes. He was not only an ally but was a personal friend of General Isaac Brock, who commanded the British troops in Upper Canada. Tecumseh played an integral part in protecting British North America from the American army. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.8-21 "Tecumseh was a renowned Shawnee" - "leader of the native peoples."

• What was Tecumseh's childhood like? What were some of the most formative events in his early years?
• The illustrations in this book are very vivid. Which ones stand out to you the most?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Parents/teachers may wish to preview this picture book for more sensitive readers. Tecumseh lived through turbulent times, and the story does not omit the loss and violence of battles. Note, if combining: some illustrations may upset younger children.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 18
lessons.body:
Tecumseh, cont.
⍞ Materials: Tecumseh

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about Tecumseh's childhood from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.22-34 "In September 1792" - "build the native confederacy."
Remember to spend time looking at the illustrations.

• What does it mean to understand land as a meal and as a "dish with one spoon"?

➜ VIEW
No portrait of Tecumseh exists from his lifetime, but this painting is thought to have been made from a sketch of an even earlier (now lost) sketch of the Shawnee chief. What do you notice about this portrait?
∞ Image Link: Portrait of Tecumseh

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 19
lessons.body:
Tecumseh, cont.
⍞ Materials: Tecumseh

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from last week's reading of the events of Tecumseh's life once he became a chief in 1795?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.36-52 "On June 18, 1812" - "native tribes of North America."
Remember to spend time looking at the illustrations.

• What was Tecumseh's role in the War of 1812? How did the end of the war affect the Indigenous peoples, who aided the British, and their lands?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING
Watch a brief video about the life of Tecumseh, which includes images of monuments and memorials to him in southwestern Ontario.
∞ Video Link: Chief Tecumseh

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 20
lessons.body:
The Métis & The Red River Settlement
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada; map or atlas of Canada that includes Manitoba

➜ INTRO
While the War of 1812 was at its height in Upper and Lower Canada to the east, a Scottish nobleman named Thomas Douglas, 5th Earl of Selkirk, was working to establish a colony on the prairies to the west, at the intersection of the Red and Assiniboine Rivers, near what is now Winnipeg. Selkirk had received a large grant of land from the Hudson Bay Company and wanted to create a farming community to support many poor Scottish highlanders and Irish peasants, who had lost their homes and livelihoods due to various upheavals at the end of the eighteenth century. This community, known as the Red River Settlement, would come to play a large role in the history of immigrants to the prairies, Métis peoples, the province of Manitoba, and the history of Canada as a nation.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.114-120 "In April 1806" - "cries of despair."

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
Look at a map or atlas of Manitoba (online is fine) and locate the Red River and Fort Douglas (present-day Winnipeg). Find the Hudson Bay on the same map so you can see the route the settlers took.
Compare this modern map to a map of the land given to Selkirk:
∞ Map Link: Map showing the Selkirk Land Grant in 1811

➜ VIEW
Learn more about elements of Métis culture, several of which are mentioned in today's reading: Métis sash, Red River cart, Red River jig, Métis flag, cuisine, beadwork, and fiddle. Follow the "Learn More" links under each heading.
∞ Link: Symbols of Métis Culture
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
First settlers arrive at the Selkirk settlement in Red River (1811)

links.text: Map showing the Selkirk Land Grant in 1811

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 21
lessons.body:
Immigration to Canada
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ INTRO
What do you know about your own family's history in Canada? Where did your ancestors come from? Where did they settle? Where in Canada do you live now?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.83 "Grosse Ile is a beautiful island" - "their home was destroyed."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING 
Read excerpts from letters Catharine Parr Traill sent to her mother detailing her first impressions of Canada in 1832. 
∞ Link: From Catharine Parr Traill, The Backwoods of Canada

links.text: From Catharine Parr Traill, The Backwoods of Canada

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 22
lessons.body:
Rebellion and Reform
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ RECAP
What did you read about immigration to Canada in the early nineteenth century?

➜ INTRO
How do we choose our government in Canada and in our provinces and territories? Do you think it has always been this way in Canada? Today we will learn about frustrations towards the government in the 1830s, which would eventually lead to the form of government we recognize today.

➜ VIEW
Remember that in 1791, the large Province of Quebec was divided into Upper and Lower Canada, roughly corresponding to the provinces of Ontario and Quebec today.
∞ Link: Upper & Lower Canada in 1791

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.93 "Bytown and Ottawa"
p.94-97 "In the 1830s" - "disguised in women's clothing"

• What changes did William Lyon Mackenzie want to see in Upper Canada?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING 
View more artwork by Cornelius Krieghoff.
 ∞ Image Link: Cornelius Krieghoff at the National Gallery of Canada 

• IMPORTANT DATES 
Louis-Joseph Papineau (1786-1871) 
William Lyon Mackenzie (1795–1861)
Rebellions in Upper and Lower Canada (1836-1837)

links.text: Cornelius Krieghoff at the National Gallery of Canada

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 23
lessons.body:
British North America
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from last week about the rebellions in Upper and Lower Canada? What were their grievances?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.97-101 "Again, British North America" - "might be possible?"

• How did the system of Responsible Government change the way British North America was governed?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING
Watch two Heritage Minutes about the reforms that were brought about by the rebellions in the 1830s:
∞ Video Link: Heritage Minutes: Baldwin & LaFontaine (Parents may wish to preview this one for younger students since it does dramatize a violent scene.)
∞ Video Link: Heritage Minutes: Responsible Government 
Queen Victoria voices her approval of the reforms.

The sailing ship Marco Polo met its fate in a storm off the coast of Cavendish, and its running aground was witnessed by a young L.M. Montgomery, who later wrote an essay about it for the Montreal Daily Witness (March 5, 1891). You can read her essay here.
∞ PDF Link: The Wreck of the Marco Polo

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 24
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.

Questions will come from:
The Story of Canada
Tecumseh

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
British North America at Mid-Century
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from the last reading about Responsible Government?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.121-127 "After the closing" - "stepping forth into the west."

• How did life for Indigenous people in the West change in the mid-nineteenth century?

➜ VIEW
In today's reading, you learned that Paul Kane's paintings "caused a sensation. No one back east had ever seen such images." Spend some time looking at more paintings by Kane.
∞ Image Link: Paul Kane: Key Works
∞ Image Link: Paul Kane at the National Gallery of Canada
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Sensitive content: parents may wish to preview the photograph on page 127 of the remains of a sailor from the Franklin expedition, preserved in the ice.

• IMPORTANT DATES
Paul Kane (1810–1871)
Arctic Expedition of Sir John Franklin and his crew (1845)
Queen Victoria chooses Ottawa as the capital city (1857)

links.text: Paul Kane at the National Gallery of Canada

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
British Columbia and the Fraser River Gold Rush
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from last week's reading or the Paul Kane picture study?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.143-151 "After Mackenzie and Fraser" - "with new interest."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Today's reading is longer, so parents/teachers may decide to add extra lesson time or save some of the reading for an afternoon.

• IMPORTANT DATES
Sir James Douglas, first governor of British Columbia (1803-1877)

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 27
lessons.body:
Pre-Confederation Years
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from last week's reading about British Columbia?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.154-161 "The 1860s were busy" - "a month later, in Quebec."

• What was discussed at the 1864 Charlottetown Conference? Who were the key players?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
Sir George-Étienne Cartier (1814-1873)
Sir John A. Macdonald (1815-1891)
George Brown (1818-1880)
Thomas D'Arcy McGee (1825-1868)
Conferences in Charlottetown and Quebec City (1864)

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 28
lessons.body:
The Battle for Confederation
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ RECAP
What do you remember about the meetings in Charlottetown from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.162-166 "Poor Mercy Ann Coles!" - "The Dominion of Canada was born."

• Canada on July 1, 1867, looked very different from what it does today. Which were the original provinces? Why did PEI not join, even after it had hosted the first meetings?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
Confederation: New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, Quebec, and Ontario form the Dominion of Canada (1867)

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 29
lessons.body:
The Loxleys and Confederation
⍞ Materials: The Loxleys and Confederation

➜ INTRO
For the next four weeks, you will read an account of the years leading up to Confederation through the eyes of a fictional family, the Loxleys, in the graphic novel The Loxleys and Confederation. Look for historical figures and places (and a piece of artwork!) that you have been learning about this year. 

➜ READ & NARRATE
Beginning with the Prologue on page 9, read for about 20 minutes, leaving time for narration. This should allow enough time to read through The Loxleys and Confederation over four weeks. Since the time to read graphic novels varies, some students will also have time to read Lillian's 1867 Diary at the end, while others can save that for afternoon occupations or an extra helping.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Parents/teachers may wish to preview the several images of battles, which might be too vivid for sensitive students.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 30
lessons.body:
The Loxleys and Confederation, cont.

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from your reading last week?

➜ READ & NARRATE
Read the graphic novel for about 20 minutes, leaving sufficient time to narrate.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 33
lessons.body:
Confederation
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ INTRO
In 1867, Canada looked very different from what it does today. It consisted of four provinces: Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Quebec, and Ontario. How and when did other provinces join Confederation? And what did this mean for the Indigenous peoples in the west and north who had lived on the land for centuries?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.166-172 "July 1, 1867" - "Riel was an exile."

• Why did some colonies choose not to join in 1867? 
• What were some effects of Canada's purchase of Rupert's Land from the Hudson's Bay Company?

➜ VIEW
Look at the map of Rupert's Land, the land sold to Canada by the Hudson's Bay Company in 1869. Which provinces and territories now exist on land that was once part of Rupert's Land?
∞ Map Link: Rupert's Land
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING
Listen to a performance of the Anti-Confederation Song, recorded only ten years after Newfoundland eventually joined Canada in 1949.
∞ Music Link: Alan Mills - Anti-Confederation Song (1959)

• IMPORTANT DATES
Louis Riel (1844-1885)
Manitoba joins Canada (1870)

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 34
lessons.body:
Into the West
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.172-177 "The Métis of Red River" - "freedom they once had."

• What were some factors that led to the founding of the North-West Mounted Police?

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 35
lessons.body:
The Great Railway
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from last week about the North-West Mounted Police?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.177-185 "On the Pacific coast" - "'great in enterprise.'"

• Why was a transcontinental railway so important, and what were some of the effects of its completion?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING
Louis Riel was tried and hanged for high treason in 1885, but history has come to regard him as, instead, one of the fathers of Confederation - the father of Manitoba. In 2024, Manitoba formally acknowledged him as their first Premier.
∞ Article Link: Riel formally recognized as first Premier

Did you notice the young boy front and centre in the photograph of the last spike being hammered into the railway at Craigellachie, BC? Learn more about him.
∞ Article Link: The Kid in the Last Spike

John A. Macdonald's wife Agnes had a unique way of riding the train! See another photograph of her from 1887.
∞ Article Link: History Corner -1887- Lady Agnes Macdonald

View a promotional poster from 1885 advertising the railroad and farming opportunities on the prairies.
∞ Image Link: The Canadian Pacific Railroad

links.text: Riel formally recognized as first Premier
The Kid in the Last Spike

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 36
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Questions will come from:
The Story of Canada
The Loxleys and Confederation

terms.content: · (Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune) Describe what life was like in Herring Cove, Nova Scotia, around 1800; consider historical details such as food, shelter, clothing, transportation, medical care, family life, communication, etc.

· (The Story of Canada and Tecumseh ) Describe Métis culture in the West: who are they, how did they live, and what was life like for them in the first decades of the nineteenth century. OR Explain who Tecumseh was and his role in the War of 1812.

· (The Story of Canada and The Loxleys and Confederation) Tell all you know about the events leading to Canada's Confederation on July 1, 1867. OR Explain why it was so important for Canada to have a transcontinental railway.
